old: Figure 2 Equilibrium transition boundaries across all nine phenological scenarios and the pairwise β×Bt parameter plane. Panels A-I show equilibrium damage D* at the stable fixed point of the annual map as each phenological parameter is varied individually across its plausible range (one-at-a-time scans; all other parameters held at the calibrated baseline). Green squares: coexistence equilibria; pink circles: parasitoid-free equilibria; blue diamonds: canopy-only (beetle-free) equilibria; teal triangles: coexistence non-fixed-point attractors. Connecting lines trace the equilibrium branch within each class. The horizontal dotted line marks the functional damage threshold. (A) Season duration T. (B) Beetle overwinter survival  . (C) Parasitoid overwinter survival . (D) Parasitoid emergence rate δ. (E) Susceptible larval mortality . (F) Passerine bird pressure index . (G) Damage-to-capacity penalty . (H) Parasitoid attack rate β. (I) Beetle annual reproduction ratio . Panel J shows the pairwise phase plane in the space; grey contours indicate  values and the bold black curve marks the  = 1 transcritical bifurcation boundary separating the coexistence regime (lower-right) from the parasitoid-free regime (upper-left). The diagonal orientation of this boundary reflects the intraguild predation mechanism: increasing bird pressure progressively raises the parasitoid attack rate required for parasitoid invasion.
new:
Figure 2: Equilibrium transition boundaries across all nine phenological scenarios (one-at-a-time scans). Panels A–I show equilibrium damage D* at the stable fixed point of the annual map as each phenological parameter is varied individually across its plausible range (all other parameters held at the calibrated baseline). Green squares: coexistence equilibria; pink circles: parasitoid-free equilibria; blue diamonds: canopy-only (beetle-free) equilibria; teal triangles: coexistence non-fixed-point attractors. Connecting lines trace the equilibrium branch within each class. The horizontal dotted line marks the functional damage threshold. (A) Season duration T. (B) Beetle overwinter survival. (C) Parasitoid overwinter survival. (D) Parasitoid emergence rate δ. (E) Susceptible larval mortality. (F) Passerine bird pressure index. (G) Damage-to-capacity penalty. (H) Parasitoid attack rate β. (I) Beetle annual reproduction ratio.

Figure 3: Pairwise β×Bt parameter plane. Grey contours indicate R_P values and the bold black curve marks the R_P = 1 transcritical bifurcation boundary separating the coexistence regime (lower-right) from the parasitoid-free regime (upper-left). The diagonal orientation of this boundary reflects the intraguild predation mechanism: increasing bird pressure progressively raises the parasitoid attack rate required for parasitoid invasion.

old: Figure 3: Parasitoid invasion threshold  as a function of each phenological parameter. Each panel shows  evaluated at the parasitoid-free fixed point of the annual map while varying one parameter across its plausible range (Table S1), with all other parameters held at the calibrated baseline. The dashed red line marks . Above this boundary, the parasitoid can invade and persist (coexistence regime); below it, the parasitoid declines to extinction (parasitoid-free regime). Vertical dotted lines indicate the approximate parameter value at which the threshold crossing occurs.

comment: Reviewer 1, Comment 2: Figure 2 split into two figures; subsequent figures renumbered.

old: If , a few parasitoids can spread and push the system to coexistence. If R_P < 1, they die out, and the system remains parasitoid-free. The  line marks where the main shift between these two states occurs. Seven of nine parameters cross the  threshold. Raising the bird pressure index  quickly lowers  and crosses the threshold. Increasing the susceptible larval death rate  also lowers  and crosses the threshold. Conversely, increasing the parasitoid attack rate β raises  and crosses the threshold. Higher  increases  with persistence around the threshold value of  from Figure 2. Beetle overwinter survival  (Figure 3B), parasitoid overwinter survival  (Figure 3C) and increasing parasitoid emergence rate δ (Figure 3D) each raises . Each threshold crossing shows the parameter value needed for persistence. Season duration T (Figure 3A) increases , so longer seasons lower but do not eliminate parasitoid persistence at baseline. The defoliation damages memory-based vernal growth  (Figure 3G), which defines carrying capacity, slightly decreases . This shows that foliage-memory feedback has minimal impact on parasitoid invasion. Therefore, Figure 3 reveals that moving from coexisting equilibria by decreasing  results from a lower attack rate, reduced beetle or parasitoid survival, or slower emergence.
new: If , a few parasitoids can spread and push the system to coexistence. If R_P < 1, they die out, and the system remains parasitoid-free. The  line marks where the main shift between these two states occurs. Seven of nine parameters cross the  threshold. Raising the bird pressure index  quickly lowers  and crosses the threshold. Increasing the susceptible larval death rate  also lowers  and crosses the threshold. Conversely, increasing the parasitoid attack rate β raises  and crosses the threshold. Higher  increases  with persistence around the threshold value of  from Figure 2. Beetle overwinter survival  (Figure 4B), parasitoid overwinter survival  (Figure 4C) and increasing parasitoid emergence rate δ (Figure 4D) each raises . Each threshold crossing shows the parameter value needed for persistence. Season duration T (Figure 4A) increases , so longer seasons lower but do not eliminate parasitoid persistence at baseline. The defoliation damages memory-based vernal growth  (Figure 4G), which defines carrying capacity, slightly decreases . This shows that foliage-memory feedback has minimal impact on parasitoid invasion. Therefore, Figure 4 reveals that moving from coexisting equilibria by decreasing  results from a lower attack rate, reduced beetle or parasitoid survival, or slower emergence.

old: Figure 4: Early-warning summary: parametric drivers, regime-shift probability, and adaptive-stability reference frame. (A) Partial rank correlation coefficients (PRCC) between each model parameter and the dominant eigenvalue magnitude ρ* across the Latin Hypercube sample; red bars indicate |PRCC| > 0.25 (warning candidates). (B) Regime-shift probability per phenological scenario (one-at-a-time variation) and for the joint Latin Hypercube sample; horizontal bars show 95% bootstrap confidence intervals; colour indicates risk level. (C) All one-at-a-time scan points projected onto the resistance–resilience plane, where resistance =  and resilience = 1 − ρ* (contrast to van Meerbeek et al. (2021) resistance and resilience); green circles denote coexistence outcomes, red crosses denote regime shifts.

comment: Reviewer 1, Comment 2: Figure 2 split into two figures; subsequent figures renumbered.

old: Figure 4 quantifies regime-shift risk and highlights the main drivers of parametric instability—variability in phenology (the timing of seasonal biological events). The partial rank correlation coefficient (PRCC) shows how sensitive system stability at coexistence is to each parameter. Stability is measured using the dominant eigenvalue magnitude ρ* (indicating how quickly the system returns to equilibrium after disturbance), based on the Latin Hypercube sample (Step 10) in Figure 4A.
new: Figure 5 quantifies regime-shift risk and highlights the main drivers of parametric instability—variability in phenology (the timing of seasonal biological events). The partial rank correlation coefficient (PRCC) shows how sensitive system stability at coexistence is to each parameter. Stability is measured using the dominant eigenvalue magnitude ρ* (indicating how quickly the system returns to equilibrium after disturbance), based on the Latin Hypercube sample (Step 10) in Figure 5A.
